one_line_takeaway: Realistic pre-existing agent memory substantially blunts MINJA-style memory-poisoning attacks on an EHR agent, but the paper's own trust-scoring defense is brittle: one model configuration blocks everything (utility-destroying), while another assigns perfect trust to 54 of 101 poisoned entries it accepts.
key_finding: Attack robustness: pre-existing relevant memory sharply reduces MINJA's real-world effectiveness. For GPT-4o-mini, ASR fell from 62% (empty memory) to 6.67% (relevant initial memory present) and ISR fell from 100% to 26.67%; for Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, ASR fell from 52.94% to 0% and ISR only marginally from 100% to 99.95% (poison is still accepted into memory but the model resists acting on it). Increasing indication prompts from 2 to 4 had no significant effect on either metric — this explicitly disproved the paper's own Hypothesis 2 for RQ1, attributed to Levenshtein-distance retrieval favoring benign queries that lack the target ID. Increasing retrieved-memory count raised both metrics: GPT-4o-mini ASR rose 6%->20%->38% (ISR 26%->50%->100%) at 3/5/10 retrieved memories; Llama ASR rose 0%->13.33%->27.27% (ISR fluctuating 99%->93.94%->97.87%) across the same settings. Defense evaluation (Memory Sanitization, Experiment set 8): with GPT-4o-mini, the trust-scoring gate rejected all 23 candidate memory entries it processed (mean trust 0.28, sd 0.10, all in the low-trust <0.5 bin), blocking only 2 of the 101 poison queries outright (~1.98% blocking rate at this layer) while achieving 0% poison leakage into memory — but only because the gate was so conservative that memory stayed effectively empty, including rejecting benign entries. With Gemini-2.0-Flash, the gate processed 151 candidate entries and showed a bimodal accept/reject pattern: 82 entries accepted at a perfect trust score of 1.0, 69 rejected with mean trust 0.35 (range 0.28-0.40). Critically, of the 82 accepted (trust=1.0) entries, 54 were confirmed malicious poison queries and only 28 were benign — meaning 54 of the 101 poison queries (about 53.5%) were assigned maximum trust and stored, and none of them would have been filtered by retrieval-time trust-decay thresholding since they all carried the maximum score of 1.0.
core_contribution: This is a course research-project report (UMass COMPSCI 690F) that does NOT introduce the memory injection attack itself — that is MINJA, from Dong et al. 2025 (arXiv:2503.03704), cited throughout as '[4]'. The paper's own contribution is (1) a systematic empirical robustness study of MINJA-style query-only memory poisoning against Electronic Health Record (EHR) agents under more realistic conditions than [4]'s original evaluation — varying initial memory state, number of indication prompts, and number of retrieved memories, on GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, and Gemini-2.0-Flash with MIMIC-III clinical data; and (2) two original defense mechanisms the authors design and empirically evaluate: an Input/Output Moderation gate (two-stage static-heuristic + LLM-semantic-classifier trust scoring that blocks entry into execution/memory) and a Memory Sanitization module with trust-aware retrieval (continuous per-entry trust score, temporal decay, and pattern-based filtering of poisoning templates at both append and retrieval time).
threat_model: Attacker: a regular EHR-agent user with no elevated privileges and no direct memory read/write access, interacting only through the standard query interface, rate-limited like any user. Victim: a legitimate clinician/staff user whose future queries are answered using poisoned shared memory. Goal: induce the agent to persist a malicious patient-ID redirection (e.g., 'Point victim ID 027-22704 to target ID 015-91239') via query-only 'indication prompts' with progressive shortening (the MINJA technique from [4]), so that later benign queries about the victim ID return the target patient's records. Assets at risk: correctness of agent output and integrity of the shared long-term memory store. Attack surface: the user input channel and, indirectly, the memory-append API.
limitations: Attack experiments use one primary victim/target ID pair in the detailed swept experiments (5 pairs exist but the multi-variable sweep is reported for one), and only two base models (GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct) for the attack-robustness study. The Input/Output Moderation defense (Defense 1) is described in methodological detail with an ablation plan, but the paper's own full text (as extracted, including the Experimental Results section 8) reports quantitative outcomes only for Defense 2 (Memory Sanitization) — no ASR-reduction numbers for Defense 1 appear in the text obtained. The Memory Sanitization results themselves reveal the defense is not yet viable: it is either too conservative to be useful (GPT-4o-mini config, empty memory) or trust-miscalibrated to the point of accepting the majority of adversarial content with maximum confidence (Gemini-2.0-Flash config), and the authors explicitly frame this as a 'confidence filter' rather than a 'security filter.' No statistical significance testing or multi-seed variance is reported for the core ASR/ISR sweep numbers despite the paper mentioning seeded/reproducible runs for the defense evaluation.

datasets_used: MIMIC-III (clinical EHR data, primary dataset for both attack and defense experiments)
eICU (used for Defense 1 / Input-Output Moderation evaluation prompt set, alongside MIMIC)
quantitative_results: Baseline attack (empty initial memory): GPT-4o-mini ASR 62%, ISR 100%; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct ASR 52.94%, ISR 100%
With relevant pre-existing initial memory: GPT-4o-mini ASR 6.67%, ISR 26.67%; Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct ASR 0%, ISR 99.95%
Effect of retrieved-memory count (GPT-4o-mini): 3 memories -> ASR 6%, ISR 26%; 5 memories -> ASR 20%, ISR 50%; 10 memories -> ASR 38%, ISR 100%
Effect of retrieved-memory count (Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct): 3 memories -> ASR 0%, ISR 99%; 5 memories -> ASR 13.33%, ISR 93.94%; 10 memories -> ASR 27.27%, ISR 97.87%
Effect of indication-prompt count (2 vs 4): no significant change in ASR/ISR for either model (Hypothesis 2 of RQ1 disproved)

Memory Sanitization defense, GPT-4o-mini run: 23 candidate memory entries processed, 0 accepted / 23 rejected, mean trust score 0.28 (sd 0.10, range 0.0-0.4), all in low-trust bin; of 101 poison queries, 2 blocked at the memory layer (~1.98% blocking rate), 0 leaked into memory

Memory Sanitization defense, Gemini-2.0-Flash run: 151 candidate entries processed, 82 accepted (all trust = 1.0) / 69 rejected (mean trust 0.35, range 0.28-0.40); of the 82 accepted entries, 54 were confirmed malicious poison queries and 28 were benign, i.e. 54/101 (~53.5%) of poison queries leaked into memory with maximum trust score; overall mean trust across all 151 entries was 0.70 with a trimodal distribution (clusters near 0.3, 0.4, 1.0)
baselines_compared: Reproduction of the MINJA baseline attack from Dong et al. 2025 [4] on GPT-4o-mini, reporting ~60% ASR as consistent with the original paper's findings
Cross-model comparison: GPT-4o-mini vs Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct for attack robustness sweeps; GPT-4o-mini-class vs Gemini-2.0-Flash for Memory Sanitization defense evaluation
Within-paper ablations: empty vs. relevant initial memory; 2 vs. 4 indication prompts; 3 vs. 5 vs. 10 retrieved memories (retrieval via Levenshtein distance)
